findings.description: StreamingMedian.add uses bisect.insort on a Python list — O(n) memmove per insert, O(n^2) total over a stream; replace with the two-heap pattern (heapq) for O(log n) adds and O(1) median, or sortedcontainers.SortedList.

StreamingMedian retains every value forever, so memory grows O(n) with stream length — acceptable for bounded batches but an OOM risk for unbounded streams; use a quantile sketch (P2/t-digest) if approximation is acceptable.

safe_mean materializes the entire Iterable via list(values) just to compute sum and len, turning an O(1)-memory single-pass mean into O(n) memory for generator inputs; use a sum+count loop or statistics.fmean.

percentile re-sorts the full dataset (O(n log n)) on every call, so computing several percentiles (p50/p90/p99) over the same data repeats identical sorts; provide a multi-percentile variant or a pre-sorted input path.
running_total's manual Python loop can be itertools.accumulate for a ~2-3x constant-factor speedup at C level; same O(n) complexity, micro-optimization only.
Sliding-window add/subtract in moving_average accumulates floating-point error over long series (the trade-off for O(n)); numeric-accuracy concern, detail deferred to critic-logic.
StreamingMedian is not thread-safe (insort is a non-atomic bisect+insert and can corrupt ordering if shared across workers); only relevant if used concurrently, and no concurrent usage is in scope.
